verses.flow: Paul, an apostle of Christ Jesus through the will of God, according to the promise of life which is in Christ Jesus,
verses.note: GREEK: "Apostolos" (ἀπόστολος, Strong's G652) denotes one sent with full authority, a commissioned envoy. "Kat' epangelian zōēs" (according to the promise of life) indicates that Paul's apostleship is oriented toward the promise of life found in Christ Jesus—the genitive "tēs en Christō Iēsou" qualifies this life as exclusively located in the Anointed One.

verses.flow: To Timothy, beloved child: Grace, mercy, peace from God the Father and Christ Jesus our Lord.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The triad "grace, mercy, peace" (charis, eleos, eirēnē) is unique to the Pastoral Epistles among Pauline salutations—most others omit "mercy" (eleos). The coordination of "God the Father and Christ Jesus our Lord" as a single source of divine blessing reflects the highest Christological claim, placing the Son alongside the Father as co-bestower of covenant blessings (cf. Titus 1:4).

verses.flow: I have gratitude to God, whom I serve from my forefathers in a pure conscience, as I have unceasingly the remembrance of you in my supplications night and day,

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb perfect middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: longing to see you, having remembered your tears, so that I may be filled with joy,

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: having received a reminder of the unfeigned faith that is in you, which dwelt first in your grandmother Lois and in your mother Eunice, and I am persuaded that also in you.
For which cause I remind you to stir into flame the gift of God which is in you through the laying on of my hands.
verses.note: GREEK: "Anazōpyrein" (ἀναζωπυρεῖν, Strong's G329) is a NT hapax legomenon meaning literally "to rekindle" or "to fan back into full flame." The prefix ana- (again) with zōpyron (live coal) creates a vivid metaphor of spiritual gift as smoldering fire requiring intentional tending. "Charisma" (χάρισμα, G5486) here denotes a divinely bestowed empowerment received through apostolic ordination.

verses.flow: For God gave us not a spirit of cowardice, but of power and of love and of sound-mindedness.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Pneuma deilias" (spirit of cowardice) stands in contrast to a threefold positive endowment: dynamis (power), agapē (love), and sōphronismos (sound-mindedness). "Sōphronismos" (σωφρονισμός, G4995) is another NT hapax legomenon, denoting self-disciplined clarity of mind. This verse grounds Christian boldness not in human resolve but in the very nature of the Spirit given by God.

verses.flow: Therefore be not ashamed of the testimony of our Lord, nor of me his prisoner, but suffer together with the gospel according to the power of God,

words.m: verb aorist active participle genitive singular masculine
verb aorist active participle genitive singular masculine
verb aorist passive participle accusative singular feminine

verses.flow: the one having saved us and having called us with a holy calling, not according to our works, but according to his own purpose and grace, the one having been given to us in Christ Jesus before eternal ages,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Pro chronōn aiōniōn" (before eternal ages) places God's salvific purpose and grace in a pretemporal decree—grace was given "in Christ Jesus" before time itself began. This echoes the divine council's eternal purpose (Eph 1:4, 3:11). TYPOLOGY: The language of holy calling (klēsis hagia) recalls Israel's election (Deut 7:6) now extended through Christ to all who believe—not by works but by divine purpose and grace.

words.m: verb aorist passive participle accusative singular feminine
verb aorist active participle genitive singular masculine
verb aorist active participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: but now having been made manifest through the appearing of our Savior Jesus Christ, who abolished death and brought to light life and incorruptibility through the gospel,
verses.note: GREEK: "Epiphaneia" (ἐπιφάνεια, G2015) carries strong connotations of a divine manifestation or royal appearing, used in Hellenistic contexts for the visible arrival of a god or emperor. Paul applies it to the incarnation of Jesus Christ. THEOLOGICAL: Christ's abolition (katargēsantos) of death and illumination (phōtisantos) of life and incorruptibility (aphtharsian) through the gospel constitutes the decisive reversal of the Adamic curse (Rom 5:12-21; 1 Cor 15:26).

verses.flow: unto which I was appointed a herald and an apostle and a teacher.
For which cause also I suffer these things; but I am not ashamed, for I know whom I have believed, and I am persuaded that he is able to guard my deposit unto that day.
verses.note: GREEK: "Parathēkē" (παραθήκη, G3866) is a legal-financial term meaning "deposit" or "that which is entrusted"—here it refers to either the gospel entrusted to God for safekeeping, or Paul's own life entrusted to Christ. THEOLOGICAL: Paul's confidence rests not in his own faithfulness but in Christ's ability (dynatos estin) to guard the deposit "unto that day" (eis ekeinēn tēn hēmeran), a reference to the eschatological Day of the Lord (cf. 2 Tim 4:8).

words.m: verb present active participle genitive plural masculine

verses.flow: Hold fast the pattern of sound words which you heard from me, in faith and love which are in Christ Jesus.

words.m: verb present active participle genitive singular neuter

verses.flow: The good deposit guard through the Holy Spirit who dwells in us.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The "good deposit" (kalēn parathēkēn) shifts from v.12—there Paul entrusts his deposit to God; here Timothy is charged to guard the apostolic deposit of sound teaching. The agent of this guarding is the Holy Spirit indwelling the community ("in us," en hēmin), indicating that doctrinal faithfulness is a pneumatological work, not merely human effort. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The Spirit's indwelling presence echoes the OT temple theology where God's Spirit dwelt among his people (Ezek 36:27; 37:14).

verses.flow: You know this, that all those in Asia turned away from me, among whom are Phygelus and Hermogenes.
May the Lord grant mercy to the household of Onesiphorus, for he often refreshed me and was not ashamed of my chain.

words.m: verb aorist middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But having come to be in Rome, he sought me out diligently and found me—
may the Lord grant him to find mercy from the Lord in that day—and how many things he ministered in Ephesus, you know very well.
verses.note: GREEK: The double use of "kyrios" (Lord) in "may the Lord grant him to find mercy from the Lord" is striking. Many scholars note that the first "kyrios" likely refers to Christ (to whom Paul prays) and the second to God the Father as eschatological judge, though the interchangeability itself reflects the high Christology of the Pastorals. HISTORICAL: Onesiphorus's search for Paul in Rome suggests Paul was not easily found, consistent with a second Roman imprisonment under harsher conditions than Acts 28.

verses.flow: You therefore, my child, be strengthened in the grace that is in Christ Jesus,
verses.note: GREEK: "Be strengthened" (ἐνδυναμοῦ, endynamoō, Strong's G1743) is a present passive imperative — Timothy is commanded to continually receive empowerment, not generate it himself. The passive voice indicates the source of strength is the grace of Christ, not human effort. THEOLOGICAL: This sets the tone for the entire chapter: faithful endurance is grounded in divine grace mediated through union with Christ Jesus.

verses.flow: and the things which you heard from me through many witnesses, these entrust to faithful men, who shall be competent to teach others also.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse establishes a four-generation chain of apostolic transmission: Paul → Timothy → faithful men → others. The term παράθου (parathou, "entrust," aorist middle imperative of παρατίθημι, G3908) is a banking term meaning to deposit something valuable for safekeeping. HISTORICAL: "Through many witnesses" (διὰ πολλῶν μαρτύρων) likely refers to the public context of Timothy's ordination or Paul's public teaching, not private instruction alone.

verses.flow: Suffer hardship together as a good soldier of Christ Jesus.
verses.note: GREEK: συγκακοπάθησον (synkakopathēson, G4777) is a compound verb: σύν (together) + κακοπαθέω (to suffer evil). This rare word emphasizes shared suffering with Paul and with Christ. The military metaphor "good soldier of Christ Jesus" (καλὸς στρατιώτης) initiates the first of three vocational analogies (soldier, athlete, farmer) in vv. 3-6.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist active participle dative singular masculine

verses.flow: No one serving as a soldier entangles himself in the affairs of life, that he might please the one who enlisted him.
And if also anyone competes, he is not crowned unless he competes lawfully.

words.m: verb present active participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: The laboring farmer must be first to partake of the fruits.
Consider what I say, for the Lord will give you understanding in all things.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: Remember Jesus Christ, raised from the dead, of the seed of David, according to my gospel,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This is a creedal summary: "raised from the dead" affirms bodily resurrection; "of the seed of David" affirms Messianic lineage and fulfillment of the Davidic covenant (2 Sam 7:12-16). TYPOLOGY: The dual emphasis on Davidic descent and resurrection echoes Romans 1:3-4, linking Jesus' human kingship with his divine vindication. The perfect passive participle ἐγηγερμένον (egēgermenon) denotes a completed action with abiding results — Christ remains in the state of having been raised.

verses.flow: in which I suffer hardship even unto chains, as an evildoer; but the word of God has not been bound.
On account of this I endure all things for the sake of the chosen ones, that they also may obtain the salvation that is in Christ Jesus, with eternal glory.
Faithful is the word: for if we died together with him, we shall also live together with him;
verses.note: STRUCTURE: Verses 11-13 form a "faithful saying" (πιστὸς ὁ λόγος), likely an early Christian hymn or confessional formula. The four conditional (εἰ) statements create a chiastic pattern: two positive promises (co-dying/co-living, enduring/co-reigning) and two sobering warnings (denying/being denied, faithlessness/God's faithfulness). TYPOLOGY: "Died together with" (συναπεθάνομεν) echoes baptismal theology of Romans 6:8, linking sacramental death with eschatological life.

verses.flow: if we endure, we shall also reign together with him; if we shall deny him, that one also will deny us;
if we are faithless, that one remains faithful, for to deny himself he is not able.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This is among the most profound statements of divine faithfulness in Scripture. God's fidelity is not contingent upon human fidelity but grounded in his own immutable nature. "To deny himself he is not able" (ἀρνήσασθαι ἑαυτὸν οὐ δύναται) — God's covenant faithfulness flows from his very being; unfaithfulness would be self-contradiction. This does not negate v. 12's warning but grounds God's judgment and mercy alike in his unchanging character.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle genitive plural masculine

verses.flow: These things remind them of, solemnly charging before the Lord not to fight about words, which is profitable for nothing, but leads to the ruin of those hearing.

words.m: verb present active participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: Be diligent to present yourself approved to God, a worker unashamed, rightly dividing the word of truth.
verses.note: GREEK: ὀρθοτομοῦντα (orthotomounta, G3718) literally means "cutting straight" — a hapax legomenon in the NT, possibly drawn from tent-making (cutting fabric straight), road-building, or stone-masonry. It denotes precision and integrity in handling the word of truth. THEOLOGICAL: The triad "approved" (δόκιμον, tested as genuine), "unashamed" (ἀνεπαίσχυντον), and "rightly dividing" establishes the standard for ministerial faithfulness.

verses.flow: But profane empty babblings avoid, for they will advance to more ungodliness,
and their word will spread as gangrene, among whom are Hymenaeus and Philetus,

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: who concerning the truth have missed the mark, saying the resurrection has already come to pass, and they overturn the faith of some.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle accusative plural masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Nevertheless the firm foundation of God stands, having this seal: "The Lord knew those who are his," and, "Let everyone naming the name of the Lord depart from unrighteousness."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The two seal inscriptions echo Numbers 16:5 LXX ("The Lord knew those who are his") from the Korah rebellion, and likely Isaiah 26:13 or Numbers 16:26. The pairing links divine election with the ethical demand for holiness. DIVINE_COUNCIL: God's sovereign knowledge of his own people (Ἔγνω κύριος τοὺς ὄντας αὐτοῦ) reflects the divine prerogative of intimate, covenantal knowing — not mere cognition but ownership and election. THEOLOGICAL: The "firm foundation" (στερεὸς θεμέλιος) stands in contrast to the crumbling faith of those deceived by Hymenaeus and Philetus.

verses.flow: But in a great house there are not only vessels of gold and of silver, but also of wood and of earthenware, and some indeed unto honor, and some unto dishonor.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle nominative singular neuter
verb perfect passive participle nominative singular neuter

verses.flow: If therefore anyone cleanses himself from these, he shall be a vessel unto honor, having been sanctified, useful to the Master, having been prepared for every good work.
verses.note: GREEK: δεσπότης (despotēs, G1203) is used here rather than κύριος — it denotes an absolute master or owner of a household, emphasizing sovereign authority over the vessels. THEOLOGICAL: Human moral agency ("if anyone cleanses himself") and divine purpose ("sanctified," "prepared" — both passive participles) are held in tension: the vessel acts, yet its sanctified and prepared state is divinely wrought.

words.m: verb present middle participle genitive plural masculine

verses.flow: But youthful desires flee; and pursue righteousness, faith, love, peace, with those who call upon the Lord out of a pure heart.

words.m: verb perfect active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But foolish and undisciplined questionings refuse, knowing that they breed quarrels.
And a bondservant of the Lord must not quarrel, but be gentle toward all, apt to teach, patient of evil,

words.m: verb present active participle accusative singular masculine
verb present middle participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: in meekness correcting those who set themselves in opposition, if perhaps God may give them repentance unto the full knowledge of truth,
verses.note: GREEK: μετάνοιαν (metanoian, G3341) — repentance — is here described as something God "may give" (δώῃ, subjunctive of δίδωμι), indicating repentance is ultimately a divine gift, not merely a human decision. THEOLOGICAL: The subjunctive μήποτε δώῃ ("if perhaps God may give") preserves genuine uncertainty about the outcome while affirming God's sovereignty over the process of conversion. This balances human responsibility (v. 21, self-cleansing) with divine initiative.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: and they may come to their senses out of the snare of the devil, having been taken captive by him unto his will.
verses.note: GREEK: ἀνανήψωσιν (ananēpsōsin, G366) means literally "to return to soberness" — the image is of awakening from a drunken stupor. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The "snare of the devil" (τοῦ διαβόλου παγίδος) reflects the adversarial role of the diábolos as a cosmic agent who entraps humans. The verb ἐζωγρημένοι (ezōgrēmenoi, G2221, "taken alive/captured") is a military term — the same word Luke 5:10 uses for "catching" men, creating a stark contrast between being captured by the devil and being captured for life by God.

verses.flow: But know this, that in the last days there shall come perilous times.
verses.note: GREEK: "Perilous" (χαλεποί, chalepoi, Strong's G5467) occurs only here and in Matt 8:28 (describing the Gadarene demoniacs as "fierce"). It denotes something difficult, dangerous, or hard to bear. "Last days" (ἐσχάταις ἡμέραις) is a technical eschatological phrase indicating the messianic age inaugurated by Christ's first coming and extending to His return (cf. Acts 2:17; Heb 1:2). THEOLOGICAL: Paul views the present church age as already participating in the "last days," not merely a future period.

verses.flow: For men shall be lovers of themselves, lovers of money, boasters, arrogant, blasphemers, disobedient to parents, unthankful, unholy,
without natural affection, implacable, slanderers, without self-control, savage, hostile to what is good,
verses.note: GREEK: "Without natural affection" (ἄστοργοι, astorgoi, G794) denotes absence of familial love (storgē), particularly between parents and children. "Slanderers" (διάβολοι, diaboloi) is the same word used as a title for the devil (G1228), literally "one who throws accusations across" — these persons take on the character of the accuser himself. "Hostile to what is good" (ἀφιλάγαθοι, aphilagathoi, G865) is a NT hapax legomenon, a compound of alpha-privative + philos + agathos.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: betrayers, reckless, puffed up with conceit, lovers of pleasure rather than lovers of God,

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb perfect middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: having a form of godliness but having denied the power thereof; and from these turn away.
verses.note: GREEK: "Form" (μόρφωσιν, morphōsin, G3446) denotes an outward shape or semblance, distinct from μορφή (morphē) which can imply inner reality (cf. Phil 2:6). Paul deliberately chooses the word for external appearance. THEOLOGICAL: This verse describes the essence of apostasy — retaining religious externals while denying the supernatural power (δύναμιν) that animates true godliness. The perfect participle ἠρνημένοι ("having denied") indicates a settled, completed rejection.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb perfect passive participle accusative plural neuter
verb present passive participle accusative plural neuter

verses.flow: For from among these are those who creep into households and lead captive idle women burdened with sins, led about by various desires,

words.m: verb present active participle accusative plural neuter
verb present middle participle accusative plural neuter

verses.flow: always learning and never able to come to a full knowledge of the truth.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And in the manner that Jannes and Jambres opposed Moses, so also these oppose the truth — men corrupted in mind, disqualified concerning the faith.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Jannes and Jambres are not named in the Hebrew Bible but were widely known in Second Temple Jewish tradition as the Egyptian magicians who opposed Moses before Pharaoh (Exod 7:11-12). They appear in the Targum of Pseudo-Jonathan, the Dead Sea Scrolls (CD 5:17-19), and Pliny. TYPOLOGY: As these sorcerers imitated Moses's signs with counterfeit power, so false teachers in the last days imitate godliness while opposing truth. "Corrupted in mind" (κατεφθαρμένοι τὸν νοῦν) is a perfect passive participle indicating a thoroughly ruined intellect. "Disqualified" (ἀδόκιμοι, G96) means failing the test, rejected as counterfeit — the same metallurgical metaphor Paul applies to himself in 1 Cor 9:27.

verses.flow: But they shall not advance further, for their folly shall be manifest to all, as also that of those men became.
But you have closely followed my teaching, my manner of life, my purpose, my faith, my longsuffering, my love, my endurance,
my persecutions, my sufferings — such things as befell me in Antioch, in Iconium, in Lystra — what manner of persecutions I endured; and out of them all the Lord delivered me.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And indeed all who desire to live godly in Christ Jesus shall be persecuted.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This is among the New Testament's most unqualified statements on suffering. The future passive διωχθήσονται ("shall be persecuted") is not conditional but declarative — persecution is the normative experience for all (πάντες) who resolve (θέλοντες, present active participle indicating ongoing desire) to live godly lives "in Christ Jesus." The prepositional phrase ἐν Χριστῷ Ἰησοῦ locates both the godly life and its resulting persecution within the sphere of union with Christ.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: But evil men and impostors shall advance from bad to worse, deceiving and being deceived.

words.m: verb perfect active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But you, remain in the things which you learned and were assured of, knowing from whom you learned them,

words.m: verb present middle participle accusative plural neuter

verses.flow: and that from infancy you have known the sacred writings, which are able to make you wise unto salvation through faith that is in Christ Jesus.
verses.note: GREEK: "Sacred writings" (ἱερὰ γράμματα, hiera grammata) is a phrase used in Hellenistic Judaism for the Hebrew Scriptures (cf. Josephus, Ant. 10.210). This is distinct from γραφή (graphē) used in v. 16, though both refer to the Old Testament. "From infancy" (ἀπὸ βρέφους) — βρέφος (G1025) denotes a newborn or nursing infant, indicating Timothy's exposure to Scripture from the earliest age through his Jewish mother Eunice and grandmother Lois (2 Tim 1:5). THEOLOGICAL: The Scriptures' purpose is soteriological — "to make wise unto salvation" — but only "through faith that is in Christ Jesus," establishing that the OT rightly read points to and culminates in the Messiah.

verses.flow: All Scripture is God-breathed and profitable for teaching, for reproof, for correction, for training in righteousness,
verses.note: GREEK: "God-breathed" (θεόπνευστος, theopneustos, G2315) is a NT hapax legomenon — a compound of θεός (God) and πνέω (to breathe/blow). It does not mean "inspired" in the modern sense of human creativity but rather "expired" — breathed out by God Himself. The word emphasizes divine origin, not human experience. "Reproof" translates ἐλεγμόν (elegmon, G1650), also a NT hapax, denoting conviction of error or refutation. THEOLOGICAL: Paul predicates θεόπνευστος of πᾶσα γραφή ("all/every Scripture"), affirming the divine origin of the entirety of the sacred writings. The four purposes — teaching, reproof, correction, training in righteousness — form a comprehensive scope: establishing truth, exposing error, restoring the fallen, and cultivating ongoing righteousness.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: that the man of God may be complete, having been thoroughly equipped for every good work.
verses.note: GREEK: "Complete" (ἄρτιος, artios, G739) is a NT hapax legomenon meaning fitted, capable, proficient — describing a state of readiness for one's task. "Thoroughly equipped" (ἐξηρτισμένος, exērtismenos, G1822) is a perfect passive participle from ἐξαρτίζω, intensifying the concept with the prefix ἐξ- (out, completely). THEOLOGICAL: The "man of God" (ὁ τοῦ θεοῦ ἄνθρωπος) echoes an OT title for prophets and servants of the Lord (1 Sam 9:6; 1 Kgs 17:18; cf. 1 Tim 6:11), placing Timothy in that prophetic lineage. Scripture's sufficiency is the ground of the minister's completeness for every good work.

words.m: verb present active participle genitive singular masculine
verb present active participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: I solemnly charge you before God and Christ Jesus, the one being about to judge the living and the dead, and by his appearing and his kingdom:
verses.note: GREEK: Diamartýromai (Strong's G1263) is an intensified form of martyréō, meaning "to solemnly witness/charge"—carrying a legal, covenantal weight. The phrase "the living and the dead" (zōntas kai nekrous) is a merism encompassing all humanity under Christ's judicial authority. THEOLOGICAL: Paul grounds his final charge in the eschatological realities of Christ's role as universal Judge and King—his epipháneia (appearing) and basileía (kingdom) forming the twin horizons of apostolic hope (cf. Acts 10:42, 1 Pet 4:5).

verses.flow: Preach the word; stand ready in season and out of season; convict, rebuke, exhort, with all longsuffering and teaching.
verses.note: GREEK: Kḗryxon (G2784, aorist imperative) means "herald" or "proclaim as a herald"—the word evokes a royal herald announcing a king's decree. Ephístēthi (G2186) literally means "stand upon" or "be at hand," conveying urgent readiness. The string of aorist imperatives (élengxon, epitímēson, parakáleson) conveys decisive, immediate action. STRUCTURE: Five sharp imperatives form a staccato rhythm in Greek, reflecting the urgency of the charge.

words.m: verb present active participle genitive singular feminine
verb present passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: For there will be a time when they will not endure the sound doctrine, but according to their own desires they will heap up for themselves teachers, having their ears tickled,
verses.note: GREEK: Hygiaínousa didaskalía ("sound doctrine") uses a medical metaphor—hygiaínō (G5198) means "to be healthy/whole." Episōreúousin (G2002) is a vivid hapax-like compound meaning "to heap up upon"—picturing an excessive accumulation of teachers. Knēthómenoi tēn akoḗn ("having the hearing tickled") uses knḗthō, meaning "to scratch/itch," found only here in the NT. THEOLOGICAL: Paul warns of a consumerist approach to teaching where desire rather than truth determines doctrine.

verses.flow: and from the truth indeed they will turn away their ears, and will be turned aside to myths.
But you, be sober-minded in all things, suffer hardship, do the work of an evangelist, fulfill your ministry.
For I am already being poured out as a drink offering, and the time of my departure has come.
verses.note: GREEK: Spéndomai (G4689) means "I am being poured out as a libation/drink offering"—a present passive indicating a process already underway. This sacrificial imagery echoes Phil 2:17. Análysis (G359) appears only here in the NT, meaning "a loosing, departure"—used of breaking camp or loosing a ship from moorings. TYPOLOGY: Paul casts his death as a priestly drink offering (cf. Num 15:5-10; Phil 2:17), framing martyrdom as liturgical sacrifice to God.

verses.flow: The good fight I have fought, the course I have finished, the faith I have kept.
verses.note: GREEK: All three verbs are in the perfect tense (ēgṓnismai, tetéleka, tetḗrēka), indicating completed action with enduring results. Agōna (G73) denotes an athletic contest or struggle. Drómos (G1408) is a racecourse (cf. Acts 20:24). STRUCTURE: The triadic structure—fight/course/faith—moves from combat to athletics to stewardship, encompassing the full scope of Paul's apostolic life.

words.m: verb perfect active participle dative plural masculine

verses.flow: Henceforth there is laid up for me the crown of righteousness, which the Lord, the righteous Judge, will render to me in that day—and not to me only, but also to all those who have loved his appearing.
verses.note: GREEK: Stéphanos tēs dikaiosýnēs ("crown of righteousness") uses stéphanos (G4735), the victor's wreath, not diádēma (a royal crown). Apókeitai (G606) means "is stored up/reserved"—a banking or treasury term. THEOLOGICAL: The crown is awarded by Christ as díkaios kritḗs ("righteous Judge"), contrasting Roman courts that condemned Paul. This reward extends to all who have agapēkósi ("have loved," perfect active participle) his epipháneia—a present love for a future reality (cf. Rev 22:20).

verses.flow: Be diligent to come to me quickly.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: For Demas forsook me, having loved the present age, and went to Thessalonica; Crescens to Galatia, Titus to Dalmatia.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Luke alone is with me. Take up Mark and bring him with you, for he is useful to me for ministry.
But Tychicus I sent to Ephesus.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: The cloak which I left in Troas with Carpus, when you come, bring—and the scrolls, especially the parchments.
Alexander the coppersmith showed me much evil—the Lord will render to him according to his works—
verses.note: GREEK: Chalkeús (G5471) means "coppersmith" or "metalworker"—appearing only here in the NT. Endeíknymi (G1731) means "to demonstrate/show forth." HISTORICAL: This Alexander may be the same figure mentioned in 1 Tim 1:20. The phrase "the Lord will render to him according to his works" echoes Ps 62:12 (LXX 61:13) and Prov 24:12, entrusting vengeance to divine justice rather than human retaliation (cf. Rom 12:19).

verses.flow: of whom you also beware, for he greatly opposed our words.
At my first defense no one stood with me, but all forsook me—may it not be counted against them—
But the Lord stood by me and strengthened me, so that through me the proclamation might be fully accomplished, and all the nations might hear; and I was delivered out of the mouth of the lion.
verses.note: GREEK: Parástē (G3936, aorist) means "stood beside"—a legal term for an advocate standing with someone in court. Enedynámōsen (G1743) means "empowered from within." TYPOLOGY: "Delivered out of the mouth of the lion" (ek stómatos léontos) echoes Ps 22:21 (LXX 21:22) and Dan 6:22, placing Paul's deliverance in the typological pattern of God rescuing the righteous from bestial powers. The lion may symbolize Satan (1 Pet 5:8), Nero, or death itself.

verses.flow: The Lord will deliver me from every evil work and will save me into his heavenly kingdom; to whom be the glory unto the ages of the ages. Amen.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Paul's confidence moves from past deliverance (v.17) to future salvation—not from physical death but "into his heavenly kingdom" (eis tēn basileían autou tēn epouránion). The doxology "unto the ages of the ages" (eis tous aiōnas tōn aiōnōn) is the strongest Greek expression of eternity, directed here to the Lord Jesus, affirming his divine status. This forms an inclusio with v.1, where Christ's kingdom and appearing frame the entire chapter.

verses.flow: Greet Prisca and Aquila, and the household of Onesiphorus.

words.m: verb present active participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: Erastus remained in Corinth, but Trophimus I left in Miletus, being sick.
Be diligent to come before winter. Eubulus greets you, and Pudens, and Linus, and Claudia, and all the brothers.
The Lord be with your spirit. Grace be with you.
verses.note: GREEK: The singular "your spirit" (tou pneúmatós sou) addresses Timothy personally, while "with you" (meth' hymōn) shifts to the plural, indicating the letter was meant to be read to the wider community. STRUCTURE: This dual benediction—one personal, one communal—forms a fitting close to Paul's final letter, balancing intimate pastoral care with ecclesial scope.
